title: Field Sampling Protocol: Apple Leaf Collection for EM-seq
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"txgf7","text":"This protocol describes a standardized method for collecting apple leaves for EM-seq DNA methylation analyses. Sampling is performed within a controlled time window around peak photosynthesis, using pooled healthy leaves preserved immediately at low temperature to ensure sample quality and consistency across sites.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: This protocol describes the field sampling procedure for collecting apple leaves to be used in an EM sequencing (EM-seq) experiment. The study includes both wild and cultivated adult apple trees sampled across multiple orchards in Europe. The same procedure is applied at every site to ensure that all samples are comparable across populations and orchards.

Each tree is sampled once, except for a small number of trees that are sampled twice. The list of trees to be sampled twice is given on the orchard map — please refer to the map before sampling. Before sampling, mark the trees that will be sampled twice with coloured ribbons so they can be identified quickly in the field. The table below must be completed for each site with the local solar time of the photosynthesis peak, the start and end times of the 3-hour sampling window (±1 h 30), and the approximate sampling date. %s %s

Leaves must be collected around the daily maximum of photosynthesis. Sampling window: 1 h 30 min before to 1 h 30 min after the peak of photosynthesis (total window of 3 hours). All samples for a given tree must be collected within this window so that the physiological state of the leaves is standardised across sites. Use the solar time table above for each site.

Sample the orchard line by line , following the rows of the orchard map strictly in order. The lines (rows) of the orchard are treated as biological replicates . This ordered sampling is critical because DNA methylation varies with the circadian cycle , and a consistent line-by-line order allows us to account for these methylation changes during downstream analysis. Record the current clock time in the field notebook approximately every 10 trees , as well as at the start and end of each row, and whenever there is an interruption. These time stamps will be linked to each tree ID at analysis.

Use a freshly cleaned punch for each tree; never share a tube between trees. Avoid leaves showing pathogen symptoms (spots, mildew, rust, necrosis) and leaves with heavy insect damage. Keep tubes cold (LN₂, dry shipper, or ice) from the moment of collection until long-term storage. Sample strictly line by line and record time on every envelope - this is required to correct for circadian methylation effects. Be aware of the risks of heatstroke and exposure to liquid nitrogen, and ensure that appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) is worn at all times. Photograph each sampled tree if possible and store the photo with the same ID as the tube.

Leaf punch (cork borer or biopsy punch), 3–6 mm in diameter Cutting mat or cutting board for biopsy punch 2 mL Eppendorf tubes (one tube per tree), pre-labelled Paper envelopes, pre-labelled, in a plastic zip-lock bag with silica gel beads 70% ethanol (for cleaning the punch between trees) Distilled water (for rising the punch after the ethanol) Small plastic pots (for ethanol and distilled water) Coloured ribbons (to pre-mark the trees that will be sampled twice) Orchard map showing the trees to be sampled, including those sampled twice Sample preservation system, one of the following (in order of preference): - Liquid nitrogen - Dry shipper charged with liquid nitrogen - Cooler box with ice (only if neither LN₂ nor a dry shipper is available) Permanent marker / labels for tube identification Field notebook for recording tree ID, site, date, and sampling time

Confirm the tree identity from the orchard map and record tree ID, country, orchard, type (wild / cultivated), date, and start time in the field notebook. Sample strictly line by line. Select 9 leaves distributed around the tree, all taken from the mid-canopy. Choose only healthy leaves free of visible pathogens (no spots, mildew, rust, necrosis, or heavy insect damage). Collect then stack the 9 leaves on top of each other and punch all of them at once with the leaf punch (3–6 mm diameter). Eject the leaf discs directly into the pre-labelled 2 mL Eppendorf tube assigned to that tree. All discs from the same tree go into the same 2 mL tube. Close the tube tightly and immediately transfer it to liquid nitrogen, a dry shipper (LN₂-charged), or ice, depending on the preservation system available at the site, in this order of preference. Before moving to the next tree, clean the punch by rinsing / wiping it with 70% ethanol to prevent cross-contamination between trees. This can be done with two pots, one with ethanol and the second as a rinsing bath with distilled water. For trees marked with a coloured ribbon, perform the sampling twice using two separate, independently labelled tubes (refer to the orchard map for the list). Place the perforated leaves in the paper envelope that corresponds to the collected tree. Write the time on the envelopes. Note any relevant observations (weather, leaf condition, pests, etc.). Move to the next tree on the same line. Only start a new line once the current line is complete.
creator: Thomas Maret
